ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pork belly food product entry; no specific braised pork belly with taro entry exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pig meat, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for braised pork belly dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for braised pork belly with taro. A0FJP (pig meat, cooked) is the closest defensible code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for braised pork belly with taro dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised pork belly with taro in Singapore is approximately 150g, comprising two to three slices of pork belly layered with taro pieces and served with braising sauce over rice.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Pork Belly With Taro In Savory Sauce — HPB Singapore Foodo Database (G00010152)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010152 for this exact dish. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork belly cooked data (NDB 10006) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat are set to -1 as not reported by HPB for this record.

gi_source.title: Taro (colocasia esculenta), boiled — GI analogous estimate; International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2008 (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB-recorded carbohydrate for this dish is 0g/100g, reflecting the meat-only analytical fraction. However, the dish contains taro, a starchy root vegetable with GI ~55 (boiled taro, University of Sydney GI Database). GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) and is classified as low per standard GI classification thresholds (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). The GI of 55 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on taro's contribution to the dish. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100; because the recorded carbohydrate is 0g per the HPB source, GL is reported as 0.0 against the recorded data, with the caveat that actual GL is higher due to taro starch not captured in the HPB record. Clinicians should treat this dish as having a meaningful glycaemic load from the taro component.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050mg/100g — far exceeds the 600mg/100g threshold; derived from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and fermented bean curd in the braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1050mg/100g represents over 70% of the 1500mg daily sodium target in a single 150g serving, and the braising sauce should not be consumed as it concentrates sodium further during prolonged cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Recorded carbohydrate is 0g/100g (meat-only analytical fraction) but taro in the dish contributes approximately 26g carbohydrate/100g of taro, raising the actual glycaemic load substantially above what the HPB record reflects
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a reduced taro portion, confirm no added sugar in the braising sauce, and account for the significant starch contribution from taro when calculating total carbohydrate intake for the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork belly is a high-fat cut with estimated saturated fat ~3.8g/100g, cholesterol at 89mg/100g, and sodium at 1050mg/100g — a triple cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to the combination of high saturated fat from pork belly, elevated dietary cholesterol, and very high sodium from the soy-based braising sauce, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly contains high arachidonic acid (omega-6) and estimated saturated fat ~3.8g/100g, amplifying pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; sodium at 1050mg/100g may worsen joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid pork belly as a high-fat, high-arachidonic-acid cut that amplifies inflammatory pathways, and should note that the high sodium content may exacerbate joint fluid retention and swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pork Belly with Taro in Savory Sauce (芋香酱意扣肉) is a classic Chinese-Singaporean braised dish featuring thick slices of pork belly layered alternately with taro pieces, slow-cooked in a rich, deeply savory braising liquid made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, fermented bean curd (腐乳), sugar, garlic, and five-spice powder. The long braising process renders the pork belly tender and succulent, with the fat partially melting into the sauce, while the taro absorbs the braising liquid and becomes soft and aromatic. The dish is characterized by its deep umami flavor, glossy dark sauce, and the contrast between the melt-in-the-mouth pork and the starchy, fragrant taro. It is commonly served at Chinese family meals, hawker centers, and zi char restaurants in Singapore, typically accompanied by steamed white rice. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal, 19.1g protein, and 10.5g fat per 100g, with sodium at a very high 1050mg/100g due to the soy-based braising sauce. The recorded carbohydrate is 0g/100g, reflecting the meat-only analytical fraction; however, the taro component contributes significant starch (approximately 26g carbohydrate per 100g of taro) that is not captured in the HPB record, meaning the actual glycaemic load of the dish is substantially higher than the recorded data suggests. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension (sodium 1050mg/100g), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium), and Type 2 Diabetes (hidden taro starch). It contains soy and fermented bean curd allergens and is not suitable for halal or kosher diets.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010152 for this exact dish with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked pork belly data as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. GI assigned as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled taro.
notes: carbohydrate_g is recorded as 0.0 per the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010152); this reflects the meat-only analytical fraction of the dish and does not capture the starch contribution from taro — confirmed zero per HPB source for the recorded fraction. sugar_g is 0.0 and dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 per the HPB record for the same reason — confirmed zero per HPB source for the recorded fraction. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported by HPB Singapore for this record; USDA FoodData Central cooked pork belly data used only for saturated_fat_g estimation. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported by HPB Singapore for this record and no approved secondary source provides complete data for this specific dish preparation. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on boiled taro (University of Sydney GI Database); GI 55 is classified as low per standard thresholds (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). The recorded GL per serving is 0.0 against the HPB-recorded carbohydrate of 0g, but actual GL is meaningfully higher due to taro starch not captured in the HPB analytical record. Clinicians should treat this dish as having a meaningful glycaemic load from the taro component.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to allowed values.
Iteration 2: Reviewed gi_category for GI 55; previous draft set 'medium' which was flagged by validator.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI 55 is the upper boundary of the low GI range per standard classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification basis explicitly.
